reference: GATE CS — Programming and Data Structures / Programming

map.condition: Counting recursive calls of naive Fibonacci fib(n)
Recurrence of the form T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)
map.use: \text{Master Theorem: compare } f(n) \text{ with } n^{\log_b a}
\text{depth} = n \text{ for linear; } \log_2 n \text{ for divide-and-conquer}

map.condition: Memoized / DP version of exponential recursion

formulas.name: Fibonacci Recurrence Definition
formulas.plain: fib(0)=1, fib(1)=1, fib(n)=fib(n-1)+fib(n-2) for n>=2
formulas.whenToUse: Base definition needed when tracing fib values or counting calls
formulas.trap: GATE uses fib(0)=1, fib(1)=1 (not 0,1); verify the base cases in the given code before computing

formulas.name: Naive Fibonacci Total Call Count
formulas.plain: Total calls for fib(n) = 2*fib(n+1) - 1
formulas.whenToUse: Counting total number of times fib is invoked (including the initial call) for naive recursive implementation

terms.means: the (n+1)-th Fibonacci number using same base-case convention

formulas.trap: Off-by-one is common; the formula uses fib(n+1), not fib(n). For fib(7) with base fib(0)=fib(1)=1: fib(8)=34, calls=2*34-1=67

formulas.name: Time Complexity of Naive Fibonacci
formulas.latex: T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + O(1) \Rightarrow T(n) = O\!\left(\phi^n\right), \quad \phi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618
formulas.plain: T(n) = O(phi^n) where phi = golden ratio ~1.618 (exponential)
formulas.whenToUse: Stating time complexity of naive (un-memoized) recursive Fibonacci
formulas.trap: Often approximated as O(2^n) in GATE options — both are acceptable; phi^n is tighter

formulas.name: Memoized Fibonacci Complexity
formulas.plain: Memoized fib: Time O(n), Space O(n) for the cache array
formulas.whenToUse: When fib uses a lookup array (f[]) to cache already-computed values
formulas.trap: The memoization check must come BEFORE the recursive calls; if placed after, no speedup occurs

formulas.name: Recursion Stack Depth for Fibonacci
formulas.latex: \text{max stack depth for fib}(n) = n \text{ frames}
formulas.plain: Maximum stack depth = n (the call chain goes fib(n)->fib(n-1)->...->fib(0))
formulas.whenToUse: Estimating stack memory needed or whether a stack overflow occurs for fib(n)

terms.means: bytes per activation record (local vars + return address)

formulas.trap: Stack depth is n, NOT the total number of calls; multiply depth by frame size to get stack bytes needed

formulas.name: Master Theorem (Divide and Conquer)
formulas.latex: T(n) = aT\!\left(\tfrac{n}{b}\right) + f(n), \quad a \geq 1,\, b > 1 \\[4pt] \text{Case 1: } f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \varepsilon}) \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a}) \\[2pt] \text{Case 2: } f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a}) \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a}\log n) \\[2pt] \text{Case 3: } f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \varepsilon}) \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(f(n))
formulas.plain: T(n)=aT(n/b)+f(n): Case1 f smaller -> Theta(n^log_b(a)); Case2 f equal -> Theta(n^log_b(a) log n); Case3 f larger -> Theta(f(n))
formulas.whenToUse: Solving recurrences of divide-and-conquer recursive algorithms where subproblem size is n/b

terms.means: cost of work done outside recursive calls

formulas.trap: Master theorem does NOT apply when subproblems have different sizes (e.g., T(n)=T(n/3)+T(2n/3)+n) — use recursion tree instead

formulas.name: Linear Decrease Recurrence
formulas.plain: T(n) = T(n-1) + constant => O(n)
formulas.whenToUse: Tail-recursive or simple recursive functions that reduce argument by 1 each call (e.g., factorial, linear search)

terms.means: constant work done at each level

formulas.trap: If work at each level is O(n) instead of O(1), result becomes O(n^2), not O(n)

formulas.name: Merge Sort / Binary Search Recurrence
formulas.plain: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) => O(n log n)
formulas.whenToUse: Merge sort, or any algorithm that splits into 2 equal halves and does O(n) merge work
formulas.trap: Master theorem Case 2 applies here (f(n)=n = n^log_2(2)=n^1); result has the extra log factor

formulas.name: Binary Search Recurrence
formulas.plain: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) => O(log n)
formulas.whenToUse: Recursive binary search, or halving with constant work per call
formulas.trap: Master theorem Case 1 applies (f(n)=1 = O(n^0), log_2(1)=0 gives n^0=1); easy to mix up with Case 2

formulas.name: Tower of Hanoi Recurrence
formulas.whenToUse: Tower of Hanoi and any recursion that doubles sub-calls while decreasing n by 1
formulas.trap: Exponential growth — 2^n moves needed for n discs; even 64 discs is astronomically large

formulas.name: Tail Recursion Definition
formulas.latex: \text{A function is tail-recursive if the recursive call is the } \textbf{last operation} \text{ in every execution path.}
formulas.plain: Tail recursion: recursive call is the very last action (no pending computation after return)
formulas.whenToUse: Determining if compiler can optimize recursion into iteration (tail-call optimization)

terms.means: Tail-Call Optimization — converts tail recursion to a loop, O(1) stack space

formulas.trap: fib(n-1)+fib(n-2) is NOT tail-recursive because addition happens after the recursive calls return

formulas.name: Stack Memory for Recursion
formulas.plain: Stack memory = max_depth * bytes_per_frame
formulas.whenToUse: Checking if recursion causes stack overflow given a fixed stack size

terms.means: bytes per activation record (locals + return address + saved registers)

formulas.trap: Each recursive call creates a NEW frame; total stack = depth * frame, not just frame size

formulas.name: Mutual Recursion
formulas.latex: f(n) \text{ calls } g(n) \text{ and } g(n) \text{ calls } f(n) \Rightarrow \text{both must have a common base case to terminate}
formulas.plain: f calls g and g calls f; termination requires a shared base case
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying and tracing programs where two functions call each other
formulas.trap: Missing or wrong base case in mutual recursion causes infinite recursion / stack overflow

formulas.name: Recursive Call Tree Node Count
formulas.latex: \text{For binary recursion with depth } d: \text{ nodes} \leq 2^{d+1} - 1
formulas.plain: Binary recursion tree of depth d has at most 2^(d+1)-1 nodes (calls)
formulas.whenToUse: Upper-bounding total calls when exact count formula is unknown

terms.means: maximum depth of the recursion tree

formulas.trap: This is an UPPER bound for balanced binary recursion; actual count may be less if branches terminate early

formulas.name: Boolean / Bit Toggle Identities
formulas.plain: For x in {0,1}: toggle x using 1-x, or XOR with 1, or (1+x) mod 2
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying equivalent Boolean-toggle statements in program tracing questions
formulas.trap: x=1+x without mod 2 does NOT toggle: if x=1, result is 2, not 0. The equivalence holds only under mod 2 or XOR context

formulas.name: Side Effects in Expression Evaluation
formulas.latex: \text{If } f(x) \text{ modifies } x \text{, then } f(x) * f(x) \neq [f(x)]^2 \text{ in general}
formulas.plain: f(x)*f(x) with side effects on x: second call sees modified x, result differs from squaring one call
formulas.whenToUse: Tracing programs where a function has side effects (modifies a pass-by-reference argument)

terms.means: function that modifies its argument x as a side effect

formulas.trap: In Pascal/C pass-by-reference, f(x)*f(x) evaluates left-to-right; x is incremented by first call, so second call uses incremented x

formulas.name: Factorial via Recursion
formulas.plain: n! = n*(n-1)! with base 0!=1
formulas.whenToUse: Tracing factorial recursive programs or computing exact call counts / output values
formulas.trap: Stack depth for factorial(n) is n+1 frames (includes the base-case frame); n frames of non-trivial work

formulas.name: GCD via Euclidean Recursion
formulas.latex: \gcd(a, b) = \begin{cases} a & b = 0 \\ \gcd(b,\, a \bmod b) & b \neq 0 \end{cases}
formulas.plain: gcd(a,b): if b=0 return a, else return gcd(b, a mod b)
formulas.whenToUse: Tracing recursive GCD programs or counting calls made by Euclidean algorithm
formulas.trap: Number of recursive calls is O(log(min(a,b))); do NOT assume O(a) or O(b) calls

formulas.name: Fast Exponentiation (Recursive)
formulas.latex: \text{power}(x, n) = \begin{cases} 1 & n = 0 \\ x^{n/2} \cdot x^{n/2} & n \text{ even} \\ x \cdot x^{(n-1)/2} \cdot x^{(n-1)/2} & n \text{ odd} \end{cases} \Rightarrow O(\log n)
formulas.plain: power(x,n): halve exponent each time => O(log n) multiplications
formulas.whenToUse: Tracing or analyzing recursive fast exponentiation (square-and-multiply) programs
formulas.trap: Naive recursion power(x,n)=x*power(x,n-1) is O(n), not O(log n); the halving trick is needed for the speedup

formulas.name: Standard Recursive Complexity Summary
formulas.latex: \begin{array}{ll} \text{Algorithm} & T(n) \\ \hline \text{Factorial / linear search} & O(n) \\ \text{Binary search} & O(\log n) \\ \text{Merge sort} & O(n \log n) \\ \text{Naive Fibonacci} & O(\phi^n) \\ \text{Tower of Hanoi} & O(2^n) \end{array}
formulas.plain: Factorial O(n), Binary search O(log n), Merge sort O(n log n), Naive Fib O(phi^n), Hanoi O(2^n)
formulas.whenToUse: Quick reference for time complexity of classic recursive algorithms in GATE MCQs
formulas.trap: Naive fib is sometimes listed as O(2^n) in GATE options — this is an acceptable upper bound even though phi^n is tighter

formulas.name: Recursion Tree Method
formulas.plain: T(n) = sum over all levels of (number of nodes at that level) * (work per node)
formulas.whenToUse: When Master Theorem does not apply or you need to verify/derive the complexity

terms.means: number of subproblems at a given tree level
work done at each node of that level

formulas.trap: Must sum ALL levels including the base-case leaf level; forgetting leaves underestimates by O(n^log_b(a))
